purpose: Copyright-safe registry of public, teacher-scored or examiner-scored writing exemplars used to calibrate routed adjudication prompts. This file stores links, score-scale metadata, and distilled decision rules only; it does not store full student responses.

copyright_policy.storage_rule: Do not store or embed full student responses from linked sources in this repository unless a separate license review explicitly permits ingestion.
copyright_policy.prompt_rule: Use distilled calibration rules and source metadata as teacher-grade anchor guidance. Do not ask models to quote, reconstruct, or rely on memorized source essays.
copyright_policy.runtime_rule: Student work being assessed must come from the active classroom workspace or from permissioned local calibration fixtures, not from copied external exemplar text.

global_anchor_rules.rule: External source rules calibrate judgment; the active classroom rubric and assignment remain authoritative for the final decision.
When the source grade differs from the active cohort, transfer the decision axis, not the absolute sophistication level.
Teacher and examiner comments consistently distinguish substance from form: organization, length, and fluency matter only when they develop the assigned meaning or purpose.
Prefer sources with multiple score points or bands for boundary decisions; single positive samples are useful as meets/exceeds anchors but weak for lower-boundary separation.
Do not copy exemplar wording into prompts. Use source-derived rules, score descriptors, and links only.

sources.name: Grade 7 Literary Analysis Task: Point of View Comparison, Sample Student Responses with Annotations
sources.rights_note: Copyright CCSSO/New Meridian. Use as public linked reference and distilled calibration guidance only.

calibration_rules.rule: Separate literary understanding from written-expression control: a paper can be organized yet still lose if it does not accurately explain how textual details support the interpretation.
Top-band literary analysis needs accurate, insightful interpretation plus carefully selected textual moments that are explained, not just named.
Logical progression and transitions support a high score only when the ideas themselves are developed and appropriate to the task.
A response that lists events or quotations without explaining how they reveal point of view, character, theme, or authorial choice should stay below essays that explain why evidence matters.

sources.name: Grade 3 Literary Analysis Task: How Illustrations Help, Sample Student Responses
sources.rights_note: Copyright CCSSO/New Meridian. Use as linked scale reference only; no response ingestion.

calibration_rules.rule: For younger grades, reward specific, accurate links between a textual or visual detail and the student's claim before expecting mature essay structure.
At elementary levels, a concise complete response with accurate evidence can outrank a longer response that drifts or repeats.

sources.name: Grade 8 Literary Analysis Task: How Dialogue Functions, Sample Student Responses
sources.rights_note: Copyright CCSSO/New Meridian. Use as linked score-scale reference only; no response ingestion.

calibration_rules.rule: Analysis of dialogue, point of view, structure, or language should explain function and effect, not merely identify the feature.
By upper middle grades, stronger papers connect textual craft to character development, conflict, or theme rather than summarizing what characters say.

sources.name: Grade 10 Literary Analysis Task: Compare and Contrast Relationships, Sample Student Responses
sources.rights_note: Copyright CCSSO/New Meridian. Use as linked score-scale reference only; no response ingestion.

calibration_rules.rule: In comparative literary analysis, a stronger response integrates similarities and differences around a meaningful claim rather than writing two disconnected summaries.
Relationship analysis should show how interactions reveal character, conflict, or theme; naming relationships alone is insufficient.

sources.name: Writing Standards in Action Grade 7: The Monsters Are Due on Maple Street
sources.provider: Massachusetts Department of Elementary and Secondary Education
sources.rights_note: Public state education resource. Store only links and distilled rules unless local licensing policy permits more.

calibration_rules.rule: A strong text-based literary response states a clear theme or interpretation and uses well-chosen action/details from the text to explain how that theme is developed.
Concise, efficient writing can be top-band when it directly answers the prompt and explains evidence; length is not a proxy for quality.
Evidence becomes strong only when the student explains the mechanism: how the detail, event, character action, or pattern supports the theme.
Published standards samples may still include imperfections; judge whether language choices support meaning before penalizing surface accuracy.

sources.name: Writing Standards in Action Grade 8: Literary Interpretation Essay
sources.provider: Massachusetts Department of Elementary and Secondary Education
sources.rights_note: Public state education resource. Store only links and distilled rules unless local licensing policy permits more.
best_use: stretch anchor for grade 7-8 literary interpretation

calibration_rules.rule: Literary interpretation should advance a defensible claim about meaning, not just recount the story or describe a character.
Narrative or contextual detail can strengthen literary interpretation when it is explicitly used to support the interpretive claim.
A stretch/top-band middle-school response explains patterns across the text and keeps the interpretation coherent across the whole piece.

sources.name: Understanding Proficiency ELA Student Work
sources.rights_note: Use as public linked teacher-scored reference. Do not copy sample responses into repository prompts.
best_use: trait calibration for evidence/elaboration, organization, and conventions

calibration_rules.rule: Separate evidence/elaboration from organization: a response with tidy structure but weak evidence or thin explanation should not beat a response with stronger developed content.
For source-based writing, reward relevant selection and explanation of source information over broad recall or copied detail.
Teacher commentary can distinguish current score from next instructional move; do not convert every fixable weakness into a ranking defeat when the core task response is stronger.

sources.name: Smarter Annotated Response Tool
sources.rights_note: Use as public linked annotated-response reference. Do not copy sample responses into local artifacts.
best_use: score-point expectations by trait and grade

calibration_rules.rule: Score and compare by the trait actually at issue; organization can be strong while evidence/elaboration remains weak, and vice versa.
A teacher-grade decision should identify the exact writing behavior that justifies the score point, not merely label the essay strong or weak.

sources.name: B.C. Performance Standards for Writing and Additional Student Samples
sources.rights_note: Public provincial education resource. Use links and distilled performance-level rules only.

calibration_rules.rule: Use four-level band language as boundary support: minimal meets, fully meets, and exceeds should differ by development, control, specificity, and effect, not by a single surface feature.
Teacher comments should cite observable writing behaviors such as originality, supporting detail, audience awareness, control, and risk-taking.
For literary or creative tasks, originality and risk-taking matter only when supported by control and enough development for the reader to understand the intended effect.

sources.name: MCAS Released Student Work and Annotations
sources.provider: Massachusetts Department of Elementary and Secondary Education

scale.label: MCAS idea development and conventions score points

sources.rights_note: Public state assessment resource. Use links and distilled score-ladder rules only.

calibration_rules.rule: Treat idea development and conventions as separable: stronger content should not be buried by conventions unless errors block meaning.
Use state score ladders to distinguish adequate development from sustained development; one accurate point is not the same as a fully developed response.

sources.name: AP English Literature Free-Response Samples and Scoring Commentary
sources.rights_note: College Board resource. Use public links and distilled rubric logic only; do not ingest student sample text.

calibration_rules.rule: Advanced literary analysis is driven by commentary: evidence volume does not compensate for weak explanation of how the evidence supports the interpretation.
A defensible thesis or claim is necessary but not sufficient; the ranking turns on the sustained evidence and commentary that follows.
Sophisticated-sounding insight should not win unless it is developed through the text; complexity without proof remains thin.

sources.name: Cambridge IGCSE Literature in English Example Candidate Responses
sources.rights_note: Cambridge resource. Use public links and distilled assessment-objective rules only; do not ingest candidate scripts.
best_use: surface meaning vs deeper meaning distinction

calibration_rules.rule: Claims about surface or figurative meaning must be rooted in textual reference plus explanation; unsupported interpretive leaps should not outrank text-grounded analysis.
Analysis of language, structure, and form should explain the effect on meaning, atmosphere, character, relationship, or reader response.
A personal or original reading is valuable only when it remains informed by the text and its context.
